title: Postpositions
situation: Navigate space, time, and relationships — Hindi's version of prepositions (but they come AFTER)

phrases.english: in the kitchen / in the bathroom
phrases.context: 'mein' = in/inside. THE most common postposition. Comes AFTER the noun, unlike English prepositions

phrases.english: on the table / on the roof
phrases.context: 'pe' (colloquial) or 'par' (formal) = on/at. 'pe' is what you'll hear in conversation 90% of the time
'ke paas' = near/with/having. Hindi has NO verb 'to have' — you say 'near him/her car is not'. Mind-bending but essential

phrases.hindi: mandir ke saamne / park ke peeche
phrases.english: in front of the temple / behind the park
phrases.context: 'ke saamne' = in front of, 'ke peeche' = behind. Two-word postpositions always use 'ke' as connector
phrases.pronunciation: MAN-dir ke SAAM-ne / PARK ke PEE-chhe

phrases.hindi: meeting ke baad / class se pehle
phrases.context: 'ke baad' = after, 'se pehle' = before. Note: 'before' uses 'se' not 'ke'. Time expressions need these
phrases.pronunciation: MEE-ting ke BAAD / CLASS se PEH-le

phrases.context: 'se' = from, 'tak' = until/to. Together they create from→to. 'ghar se office tak' = from home to office
'ke liye' = for (purpose/benefit). With pronouns: 'mere liye' (for me), 'uske liye' (for him/her)

phrases.english: by bus / by auto / on foot
phrases.context: 'mein' for enclosed vehicles (bus/train/car), 'se' for open vehicles (auto/bike) or means. 'paidal' = walking

phrases.hindi: is kaam ke baare mein socho
phrases.context: 'ke baare mein' = about (topic). Literally 'in the matter of'. Very common in conversation
phrases.pronunciation: IS kaam ke BAA-re mein SOO-cho

phrases.context: 'ke bina' = without, 'ke saath' = with (accompanying). 'mere saath chal' = come with me

grammar_notes: POSTPOSITIONS come AFTER the noun (unlike English prepositions). English: 'in the house', Hindi: 'ghar mein' (house-in). Always noun + postposition
Simple postpositions: mein (in), pe/par (on/at), se (from/by/with), tak (until/to), ko (to/object marker)
Compound postpositions use 'ke/ki' as connector: ke paas (near), ke saamne (in front), ke peeche (behind), ke upar (above), ke neeche (below), ke baad (after), ke liye (for)
The connector changes with gender: 'ke' (masculine), 'ki' (feminine). 'Ghar ke saamne' but 'dukaan ki taraf' (toward the shop-feminine)
Pronouns change form before postpositions: main→mere/mujh, tu→tere/tujh, woh→uske/us, hum→hamare/hum. This is called the oblique case
culture_notes: Hindi has NO verb 'to have'. Possession is expressed as 'near me [thing] is': 'mere paas paise hain' = I have money (literally: near-me money is)
Directions in India use landmarks, not street names: 'mandir ke saamne' (in front of the temple), 'nadi ke us paar' (across the river)

skill_breakdown.explanation: Hindi uses postpositions (after the noun) instead of prepositions. 'ko' = to/for (direct/indirect object), 'se' = from/with/by, 'mein' = in/inside, 'par' = on/at.

more_examples.english: the book is on the table

skill_breakdown.explanation: Hindi possessive agrees with the OBJECT possessed, not the owner. 'mera bhai' (my brother, masc) vs 'meri behen' (my sister, fem). 'X ka' (masc), 'X ki' (fem), 'X ke' (masc-plural / before postposition).

more_examples.hindi: Rahul ka ghar bahut bada hai

skill_breakdown.skill: Compound postpositions (ke baare mein, ke saath, ke liye)
skill_breakdown.explanation: Combine 'ke' with another word: 'ke baare mein' (about), 'ke saath' (with), 'ke liye' (for), 'ke paas' (near / in possession of), 'ke peeche' (behind). Always uses oblique 'ke' regardless of gender.

more_examples.hindi: aapke baare mein bata rahe the

more_examples.english: I don't have money (lit: near me money is not)

practice_prompt: You are a Hindi tutor drilling postpositions — `mein`, `par`, `se`, `ko`, the `ka/ki/ke` possessive cluster, and compound postpositions like `ke saath`, `ke liye`, `ke baad`, `ke paas`. Use `aap` throughout. Open with `chaliye postposition practice karte hain — bataaiye Hindi mein: 'The book is on the table' — aapka jawab?` Then run 6-8 mixed items: some are English sentences for the user to translate (`'I went to the market with Ram'`, `'Papa's car is in front of the house'`); some are noun-plus-relationship fill-ins (`Ram + at home → Ram __ ghar __`); some test oblique shifts on `-aa` nouns (`ladka` → `ladke ko`, `kamra` → `kamre mein`). Mix simple postpositions, the `ka/ki/ke` agreement with the possessed noun, and compounds. Watch closely for PRE-positioning (English habit of putting the postposition first), missing oblique case on `-aa` nouns, wrong gender agreement on `ka/ki/ke`, and `mein` vs `par` confusion. Flag every mistake with `[[CORRECTION: original="..." correct="..." reason="..."]]`. Don't switch to English unless asked. End with `bahut khoob, aapne acche se kiya — phir milte hain`.

theory.intro: In Hindi, what English calls a 'preposition' comes AFTER the noun and is called a 'postposition'. Same idea, opposite order. There's a small set of simple postpositions (in, on, from, to) and a larger set of compounds (with, for, after, about) built on top of them. The trickier part: the possessive postposition `ka/ki/ke` shifts form to agree with the possessed thing, and nouns ending in `-aa` change shape (the 'oblique case') whenever any postposition is attached. Once those mechanics click, postpositions become predictable.

sections.heading: What's a postposition (and why 'post')
sections.body:
English puts its little relationship words BEFORE the noun: `in the house`, `with Ram`, `on the table`. Hindi puts them AFTER. The noun comes first, then the relationship word. `In the house` becomes `ghar mein` — literally `house in`. `With Ram` becomes `Ram ke saath` — literally `Ram of-with`.

The job is identical to an English preposition: marking location, direction, time, possession, accompaniment. Only the word order flips. Because they come AFTER the noun rather than BEFORE, grammarians call them postpositions. Same tool, opposite end of the noun.

examples.breakdown: ghar (house) + mein (in) — English would say 'in house', Hindi flips it
Ram + ke saath (with) — Ram comes first, the 'with' attaches after
Delhi + se (from) — the 'from' lives at the end

callout.body: A 'postposition' is just 'a preposition that comes after the noun'. Same job (marking location, direction, possession, etc.), opposite location in the sentence. English speakers' instinct to put it first is the #1 thing to retrain.

sections.cutting_intro: Hi! Let's start with the big switch. In Hindi, the little words like 'in', 'on', 'with' come AFTER the noun, not before. Watch.

quick_check.question: How would Hindi say `on the table`?
quick_check.explanation: Correct! `mez par` — noun first, then the postposition. English `on the table` flips to Hindi `mez par` (table-on).

sections.heading: The simple four: mein / par / se / ko
sections.body:
Start with these four — they cover most of what English does with `in / on / from / to`. They're single-word postpositions: just attach them after the noun and you're done. No agreement, no shape change for these four themselves.

The one to watch is `se`. It's the busiest postposition in Hindi — it does the work of English `from`, `by` (means/instrument), `with` (in some idioms), and `since` (for time). Don't try to translate it as one English word; learn each use as a pattern.

table.caption: The four core simple postpositions
rows: Delhi se (from Delhi), chammach se (with a spoon)

callout.body: `se` is the most overloaded postposition. It marks the source (`Delhi se` = from Delhi), the instrument (`chammach se khaao` = eat with a spoon), the means of transport (`bus se` = by bus), and 'since' in time (`kal se` = since yesterday). Whenever you'd say `from`, `by`, or `with-using` in English, Hindi probably wants `se`.

sections.cutting_intro: Four postpositions cover most of daily Hindi. Memorize these and you can already say where things are and where they're going.

quick_check.question: Which postposition would you use for `by bus` (means of transport)?
quick_check.explanation: Yes — `bus se` = by bus. `se` covers from, by, and with-instrument. `bus mein` would mean inside the bus (location), which is a different idea.

sections.heading: The possessive cluster: ka / ki / ke
sections.body:
The possessive postposition `ka` means `of` or apostrophe-s — but it changes form to agree with the THING possessed, not the owner. `Ram ka kutta` (Ram's dog) uses `ka` because `kutta` (dog) is masculine. `Ram ki billi` (Ram's cat) shifts to `ki` because `billi` (cat) is feminine. `Ram ke bachhe` (Ram's children) shifts to `ke` because `bachhe` is plural masculine.

This is the exact same agreement pattern as -aa adjectives from the noun-gender chapter. The form depends on what comes AFTER, not before. `Ram` (the owner) doesn't care — `ka/ki/ke` is tracking the gender and number of the noun on its right.

table.caption: ka / ki / ke — which form goes with what

callout.body: Quick mnemonic: ka/ki/ke follows the same -aa/-i/-e shape as accha/acchi/acche. If you can do adjective agreement, you can do possessive agreement — it's the same machinery.

sections.cutting_intro: Now the possessive. `Ka` means `of`, but it shifts form to agree with the thing being possessed. Same trick as adjectives.

quick_check.question: How would you say `papa's car` (gaadi is feminine)?
quick_check.explanation: Correct! Gaadi is feminine, so the possessive shifts to `ki`. The form tracks the thing owned, not the owner.

sections.heading: Compound postpositions: ke + word
sections.body:
Beyond the simple four, Hindi builds a whole library of more specific relationships by combining `ke` with another word. `ke saath` = with (accompanying). `ke liye` = for. `ke baad` = after. These are called compound postpositions and they're how Hindi expresses everything English does with `with`, `for`, `near`, `about`, `before`, `after`.

The `ke` at the front is the oblique form of `ka` and stays as `ke` regardless of gender for these compounds. (A few use `ki` instead — like `ki taraf` = toward — but `ke` is the default.) The whole two-word unit goes AFTER the noun.

table.caption: Common compound postpositions
rows: store ke saamne (in front of the store)

callout.body: `ke paas` does double duty as `have`. Hindi has no verb `to have` — instead you say `near me [thing] is`: `mere paas paisa hai` literally = `near-me money is`, but translates as `I have money`. Same for everyone: `uske paas time nahi hai` = `he/she doesn't have time`.

sections.cutting_intro: Beyond the simple four, Hindi builds whole new postpositions by combining `ke` with another word. This is how you say `with`, `for`, `after`, `about`.

quick_check.question: Which compound would you use for `after eating`?
quick_check.explanation: Yes — `khaane ke baad` = after eating. `ke saath` = with, `ke liye` = for, `ke baad` = after.

sections.heading: The oblique case: -aa nouns shift before a postposition
sections.body:
Here's the trap. When you attach ANY postposition to a noun ending in `-aa`, that `-aa` changes to `-e`. `ladka` (boy) becomes `ladke ko` (to the boy). `kamra` (room) becomes `kamre mein` (in the room). `bachcha` (child) becomes `bachche ke saath` (with the child). This shape change is called the oblique case.

It only happens to `-aa`-ending masculine nouns (and pronouns — `main` becomes `mujhe/mere`, `tu` becomes `tujhe/tere`, etc.). Nouns ending in other vowels (`-i`, `-u`) or consonants don't change shape: `ghar mein` (not `ghare mein`), `kitaab par` (not `kitaabe par`). The oblique only bites `-aa` nouns.

table.caption: Direct form vs oblique form (before any postposition)

callout.body: Forgetting the oblique shift is the single most common postposition error English speakers make. `ladka ko` is wrong — it has to be `ladke ko`. Drill the shift `-aa → -e` until it's automatic. Other endings are safe; only `-aa` masculines flip.

sections.cutting_intro: One last twist — the catch that trips up everyone. When a postposition latches onto an `-aa`-ending noun, the noun itself changes shape. Listen up.

quick_check.question: Which is correct: `in the room` (kamra = room)?
quick_check.explanation: Correct! `kamra` ends in `-aa`, so it shifts to `kamre` before the postposition. The oblique case in action.

theory.wrap_up: Postpositions are everywhere in Hindi sentences, and English speakers' instinct to put them BEFORE the noun is the first thing to unlearn. Master the simple four (`mein`/`par`/`se`/`ko`), then the possessive cluster (`ka/ki/ke` with its agreement), then the compounds (`ke saath`, `ke liye`, etc.). The oblique case on `-aa`-ending nouns is the trap to watch for — `ladka` becomes `ladke`, `kamra` becomes `kamre`, every time. The phrases below give practice in all of these.
